title: The Mole Concept
subtitle: Counting particles without counting particles
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Use the mole as a counting unit for particles
Relate amount of substance to number of particles using Avogadro's constant
lessonIntent: Students can explain why the mole is used and convert between moles and particles.
examSkill: Choose and apply N = n x N_A or n = N / N_A with correct units.

scenes.caption: The Mole Concept — Year 11 Chemistry, Module 2

voiceover.text: The Mole Concept. Year eleven Chemistry, Module two.

scenes.heading: How do chemists count atoms they cannot see?
scenes.body: How do you count atoms when you cannot see or weigh one atom?
scenes.caption: The mole lets chemists count invisible particles using a measurable amount.

voiceover.text: OK, here's a question that sounds impossible at first. How do chemists count atoms — when a single atom is way too small to ever see? The answer's actually beautifully simple. It's a counting unit called the mole. By the end of this lesson, you'll be using it like second nature.

scenes.heading: Atoms are impossibly small
scenes.body: A single carbon atom weighs two times ten to the negative twenty three grams — far too small to measure on any lab balance. Think of a mole like a dozen: both are counting units that let us work with manageable numbers. Where the analogy breaks: a dozen is always twelve, but a mole is six point zero two two times ten to the twenty three — a number so large it bridges the atomic scale to the laboratory scale.
scenes.secondary: Yet reactions need exact ratios: 2 H atoms per O atom to make water. Chemists needed a bridge.
scenes.callout: This is the problem the mole solves.
scenes.caption: Atoms are too small to count individually — chemists needed a counting bridge.

voiceover.text: Let's start by appreciating just how small atoms are. A single carbon atom weighs about two times ten to the minus twenty-three grams. That's so tiny no lab balance on Earth could ever measure it. So chemists do what bakers do — they count in groups. A dozen eggs gets you twelve. A mole of atoms gets you six-point-zero-two-two times ten to the twenty-three. Same idea, just on a totally different scale. And it's that scale that bridges the atomic world to anything you can actually weigh out in the lab.

scenes.heading: One mole = 6.022 × 10²³ particles
scenes.body: One mole is the amount of substance containing exactly 6.022 × 10²³ elementary entities.
scenes.secondary: Those entities can be atoms, molecules, ions, or formula units — whatever you are counting.
scenes.caption: One mole contains exactly 6.022 × 10²³ particles — this is Avogadro's number.

voiceover.text: Alright, here's the actual definition you need to lock in. One mole is the amount of substance containing exactly six-point-zero-two-two times ten to the twenty-three particles. Those particles can be anything you're counting. Atoms, if you're dealing with an element. Molecules, if it's a compound. Or ions and formula units, depending on the substance. The number itself has a name: Avogadro's number. And its symbol is capital N with a little A subscript. You'll see that symbol everywhere in this module, so get comfortable with it now.

scenes.heading: How big is 6.022 × 10²³?
scenes.body: Avogadro's number of sand grains would cover all of Australia 100 metres deep.
scenes.secondary: The number of stars in the observable universe (~10²³) is still 10× smaller than Nₐ.
scenes.caption: Avogadro's number sits above the number of stars in the observable universe.

voiceover.text: Now let's actually appreciate how big Avogadro's number is, because the digits don't really land until you picture them. If you had Avogadro's number of grains of sand, they'd cover the entire continent of Australia to a depth of one hundred metres. Compare that to the number of stars in the observable universe? About ten to the twenty-three, still ten times smaller. And the number of seconds since the Big Bang? Only four times ten to the seventeen. Avogadro's number is genuinely one of the largest numbers you'll meet anywhere in science.

scenes.heading: Why 6.022 × 10²³ specifically?
scenes.body: One mole of carbon-12 atoms has a mass of exactly 12 grams.
scenes.secondary: This links atomic mass to real mass: molar mass in g/mol equals relative atomic mass from the periodic table.
scenes.callout: This is why the periodic table is useful.
scenes.caption: Nₐ was chosen so that 1 mol of carbon-12 has a mass of exactly 12 grams.

voiceover.text: So a fair question, why this particular number? Why six-point-zero-two-two times ten to the twenty-three, and not say ten to the twenty-four? Well, it wasn't random. The mole was defined so that exactly one mole of carbon-twelve atoms weighs exactly twelve grams. That was a deliberate design choice, and it pays off massively. Because of it, the molar mass of any element in grams per mole equals its relative atomic mass straight from the periodic table. Take hydrogen, atomic mass one, so molar mass one gram per mole. Same logic for carbon: twelve on the table, twelve grams per mole. Same for oxygen: sixteen and sixteen. The pattern holds for every element. No conversion required.

scenes.heading: N and n are not the same
scenes.body: Wrong instinct: Capital N and lowercase n are interchangeable — both tell you how much you have. Corrected rule: Capital N is the raw count of particles — a huge number with no units. Lowercase n is the amount in moles — a countable quantity with units. They are related by N = n × N_A, but they are not the same thing. In HSC questions, read carefully: does the question ask for N (number of particles) or n (amount in moles)?
scenes.secondary: Lowercase n is the amount in moles — a small number. n = 2 mol → N = 1.204 × 10²⁴ particles.
scenes.callout: Ask first: am I finding N or n?
scenes.caption: N (particles) and n (moles) are completely different — never swap them.

voiceover.text: Here's a trap that catches half the class on their first mole question. Students assume capital N and lowercase n are the same thing — both just 'how much you have'. They're not. Capital N is the raw count of particles — a huge number, no units. Lowercase n is the amount in moles. They're linked by N equals n times Avogadro's number, but they're completely different quantities. So before you do anything on any HSC question, read carefully. Does it want capital N, or lowercase n? Get that wrong, and the whole answer's wrong.

scenes.heading: N = n × Nₐ
scenes.secondary: N = particles (no units)  ·  n = moles (mol)  ·  Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹. Units cancel: mol × mol⁻¹ = dimensionless count.

unitCancel.result: N is a particle count, so no mol unit remains.

scenes.caption: N = n × Nₐ connects particle count, moles, and Avogadro's number.

voiceover.text: OK, this is the formula that ties the whole module together. N equals n times Avogadro's number. There are three quantities here. Capital N, that's the number of particles, a raw count. Lowercase n, that's the amount in moles. And Avogadro's number is the bridge between them. The trick: know any two of these, and you can find the third just by rearranging. And notice the units. Moles times moles to the minus one gives you a pure number. That's why N has no units on its answer, it's just a count.

scenes.heading: Finding N — number of particles
scenes.question: How many atoms are in 2.5 mol of carbon?
scenes.coachNote: Target: N. So multiply by Nₐ.
steps: Answer: N = 1.506 × 10²⁴ = 1.5e24 atoms. (mol cancels with mol⁻¹).
scenes.caption: N = 2.5 × 6.022 × 10²³ = 1.506 × 10²⁴ atoms.

voiceover.text: Alright, let's work through our first example together. The question reads: how many atoms are in two-point-five moles of carbon? So how do we attack this? The first move I always make, and it honestly wins half the marks, is figure out what we've been given. We've got n, the moles, equal to two-point-five. And we know Avogadro's number, which is the constant six-point-zero-two-two times ten to the twenty-three per mole. Now what are we asked for? We want N, the number of atoms. So we need the formula that links them: N equals n times Avogadro's number. From here, it's pure substitution. We plug in our n value of two-point-five, multiply by Avogadro's, and the calculator gives us one-point-five times ten to the twenty-four atoms. Quick sanity check: that's about one-and-a-half times Avogadro's number, exactly what you'd expect from one-and-a-half moles. And notice how the moles cancel with the per mole on Avogadro, leaving just a count of atoms, no units. That's how you know the setup was right.

scenes.heading: Finding n — amount in moles
scenes.question: A sample contains 3.01 × 10²⁴ molecules of water. How many moles is this?
scenes.coachNote: Target: n. So divide by Nₐ.
scenes.caption: Rearrange to n = N ÷ Nₐ when you need the amount in moles.

voiceover.text: Now let's flip the problem. The question reads: a sample contains three-point-zero-one times ten to the twenty-four molecules of water. How many moles is this? OK, so what's different here? Last time we knew n and wanted N. This time we know N and we want n. Same formula still applies, N equals n times Avogadro's, we just rearrange it. n equals N divided by Avogadro's number. Substitute the numbers in, and you get exactly five-point-zero moles. Quick unit check: particles divided by particles per mole leaves moles. That's exactly what we wanted. So the same formula works both directions, you just rearrange depending on what you're chasing.

scenes.heading: Two mistakes to fix right now
scenes.body: Mistake 1: swapping N and n. Always label which quantity you are finding before you substitute.
scenes.secondary: Mistake 2: dropping units. Nₐ is mol⁻¹. Write units at every step — confirm they cancel to what you expect.
scenes.callout: The units tell you if the setup is wrong.
scenes.caption: Always label N or n before substituting, and write units at every step.

voiceover.text: So let's talk about two mistakes that'll cost you marks, fix them now and you save yourself in the exam. The first one: confusing capital N with lowercase n. We just talked about this, capital N is a raw count, no units. Lowercase n is in moles. Before you substitute anything, write down which quantity you're solving for. Literally just label it. Now the second mistake: dropping the units of Avogadro's number. It's measured in moles to the minus one, basically per mole. When you multiply moles by that, the units cancel beautifully and leave you with a pure count. But if you don't track them, you won't notice when something's off. So write units at every single step, they're your safety net.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: A sample contains 0.75 mol of NaCl. How many formula units does it contain?
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: are you finding N or n?
scenes.caption: N = 0.75 × 6.022 × 10²³ = 4.52 × 10²³ formula units.

voiceover.text: OK, time for you to try one. The question: a sample contains zero-point-seven-five moles of sodium chloride. How many formula units does it contain? Pause now, work it out, then come back when you're ready. So, we've got n equals zero-point-seven-five moles, and Avogadro's number is the usual constant. We want N, so the formula's N equals n times Avogadro's. Substitute the numbers in, and you get about four-point-five-two times ten to the twenty-three formula units. If you got that, you're set for this topic.

scenes.heading: The Mole Concept — Key Ideas
scenes.finalPrompt: Before every question: N or n?
points: The mole is a counting unit: 1 mol = 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number, Nₐ).
Capital N = number of particles (no units). Lowercase n = amount in moles.
Formula: N = n × Nₐ. Know any two, rearrange to find the third.
Units always cancel: mol × mol⁻¹ gives a dimensionless particle count.
Nₐ was defined using carbon-12 so molar mass (g/mol) equals atomic mass from the periodic table.
scenes.caption: 1 mol = 6.022 × 10²³. Use N = n × Nₐ. Units always cancel.

voiceover.text: OK, let's lock in what we've covered. Five things to take away. First, the mole is just a counting unit, one mole equals Avogadro's number of particles. Next, don't confuse capital N with lowercase n. Capital N is the count of particles, no units. Lowercase n is the amount in moles. The formula that links them is N equals n times Avogadro's, and you can rearrange it to find any of the three quantities. The units always cancel beautifully, moles times moles to the minus one leaves you with a pure count. And the reason the whole system works: Avogadro's number was defined using carbon-twelve, so the molar mass in grams per mole equals the atomic mass straight off the periodic table. That's the foundation the rest of the module builds on.
